input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, characterized by the integration of static design models with real-time operational data for simulation, analysis, and optimization across its entire lifecycle from initial design to operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real-time monitoring of operational data, predictive analytics for maintenance and performance forecasting, and optimization of infrastructure management to support data-driven decision-making for AECO stakeholders throughout the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation utilizes Building Information Modeling (BIM) for structured data management, integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time IoT data exchange and device management, and leverages Unreal Engine for high-fidelity interactive visualization and simulation environments.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple operational digital twin instances are managed through a centralized DT Framework acting as a hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange for holistic building lifecycle management, with roles including Building Control Engineers for device modeling, Facility Managers for monitoring/control, and DT Engineers for service integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points supports integration of custom services and analytics, runtime configuration management enables adjustments without full redeployment, and the DT Framework acts as a central hub coordinating roles for holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: BIM-based geometric and semantic models for Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) defined by Building Control Engineers. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are used to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models for automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts. Real-time data from physical building sensors and actuators is synchronized with the digital twin through continuous data exchange capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators and controllers are modeled and configured by Building Control Engineers within the BIM environment, with control logic defined to enable precise actuation of physical systems. The framework translates control signals into actionable commands for physical infrastructure components, ensuring accurate and responsive operation through standardized extension points for custom services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined and configured within the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers, with their spatial distribution and operational parameters systematically embedded into the digital model. Live operational data from these sensors is collected and synchronized in real-time to support monitoring and control functions of the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is quantified using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to assess alignment between the digital twin and physical counterpart. Validation processes include systematic checks during model transformation from BIM and runtime operations, with error detection and correction mechanisms ensuring continuous accuracy and reliability of the twin's representations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from physical assets is transmitted via Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time synchronization, enabling continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands from the digital twin are sent to Building Control Systems (BCS) via Eclipse Hono and Ditto, allowing facility managers to operate physical infrastructure with bidirectional feedback mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle comprises initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers, automated transformation via Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to generate deployable artifacts, real-time operational synchronization through continuous data exchange and interactive visualization for Facility Managers, and evolution via modular extension points supporting custom services developed by DT Engineers to address changing operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to transform enriched BIM models into structured representations, automatically generating deployable Digital Twin artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points, enabling DT Engineers to integrate custom services and analytics for adaptive operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data synchronization and interactive visualization enable continuous monitoring and control of physical infrastructure. Decision-making is supported by custom analytics services integrated through modular extension points, though specific analytical techniques, insight generation methods, or confidence metrics for recommendations are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: BIM is established as a standard solution in the construction industry, yet the absence of standardized integration frameworks for Digital Twins necessitates our framework's development. The solution implements standardized extension points to ensure modular interoperability of custom services and analytics, addressing critical gaps in DT standardization.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

It correctly identifies the domain (AECO), the physical asset (a building), the methodology (integration of static models with runtime data), the purpose (simulation, analysis, optimization), and the lifecycle scope. Supported by: 'integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization'; 'complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The description aligns well with the specific services listed for Digital Twins in the AECO domain within the source text. It correctly identifies monitoring, predictive analytics, optimization, and decision support capabilities. Supported by: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization'; 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'; 'data-driven infrastructure management and optimization'.

This extraction is highly accurate, listing BIM, Eclipse Hono/Ditto, and Unreal Engine with their correct functionalities as described in the abstract and introduction. Quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', and 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The extraction is well-supported by the source, describing the centralized framework and specific roles. Evidence includes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management' and Table I which defines roles like 'Building Control Engineer', 'Facility Manager', and 'DT Engineer'. The extraction is highly specific and grounded in the text, covering architecture, extensibility, and coordination. The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on internal framework architecture, BIM integration, and the use of middleware (Hono/Ditto) for connectivity. While it mentions 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' as a non-functional requirement, it does not describe specific integration patterns with external Digital Twins, enterprise systems, or data exchange protocols for horizontal integration as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the assessment is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

This is strongly supported by the text: 'enabling building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models', and 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The description correctly states that actuators and control logic are modeled by Building Control Engineers within BIM. It also accurately reflects the framework's capability to translate signals and its standardized extension points. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineers... defines the control logic' and 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The extraction aligns with the text regarding the configuration of sensors by Building Control Engineers in BIM and the synchronization of live data. It correctly links these components to the digital twin's monitoring functions. Supported by quotes: 'building control engineers to define sensors... directly within the BIM environment' and 'runtime aspects, such as sensor configurations... and live operational data'.

The extraction is highly specific and grounded in the text, correctly identifying the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric for fidelity and mentioning validation steps during model transformation. Supported by quotes: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and 'after validating the model [65], the model is fed into the Hono/Ditto Configurator'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
